card.title: Why Familiarity Breeds Affection
card.teaser: You're more likely to fall for the person sitting next to you in class than the most attractive stranger across the country.
card.coverImagePrompt: Two strangers at adjacent café tables catching each other's reflection in a rain-speckled window, soft warm light, cinematic, painterly intimacy.

lessons.title: A silent experiment
lessons.body: In 1968, psychologist Robert Zajonc planted four women in a college lecture hall. They looked similar, sat quietly, never spoke, and simply attended a different number of classes — one attended 15, another 10, another 5, and one didn't show up at all. At the end of the term, students rated photos of all four. The more classes a woman had silently attended, the more attractive and likeable students rated her. They weren't even sure they recognized her.

visual.imagePrompt: A lecture hall from the back, one empty seat among many, soft beam of light falling on it

lessons.title: The mere exposure effect
lessons.body: Zajonc's finding — that repeated exposure to something, even without interaction, makes us like it more — became known as the mere exposure effect. It works on faces, songs, words, shapes, even nonsense syllables. Evolutionarily, the logic is simple: things we've encountered before and survived are probably safe. Familiarity becomes a shortcut for 'good'.

keyTerms.definition: A tendency to develop a preference for things simply because we've been exposed to them repeatedly

visual.imagePrompt: A face slowly coming into focus across four sequential frames, each clearer and warmer than the last

lessons.title: Proximity beats perfection
lessons.body: A 1950s MIT housing study by Festinger, Schachter, and Back found that residents of an apartment complex overwhelmingly befriended the neighbors closest to them — not the ones they had most in common with. Simply living two doors down versus five doubled your odds of becoming close friends. Decades of follow-up research show the same thing in workplaces, classrooms, and dating apps: proximity and repetition quietly shape who we love.
lessons.funFact: One study found people rated their own mirror-image photo as more attractive than the real one — because that's the face they saw every morning.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1950s apartment building seen from above, golden threads connecting neighboring doors but not distant ones

lessons.title: The quiet design of your love life
lessons.body: We like to believe attraction is driven by sparks, chemistry, and fate. The research says something humbler: most of the people we love, we love partly because they kept showing up. The practical implication? If you want more meaningful connections, optimize for repeated, low-pressure contact with the kinds of people you want in your life — communities, classes, regular places — rather than hunting for perfect strangers.

quiz.question: What did Zajonc's silent classroom experiment demonstrate?
quiz.explanation: The women never interacted with students — yet those seen more often were rated more likeable.

quiz.question: What did the MIT housing study find to be the strongest predictor of friendship?
quiz.explanation: How close neighbors lived to each other mattered more than almost any personal trait.

quiz.question: Why might familiarity have evolved to feel attractive?
options: Our brains prefer novelty but fake it
quiz.explanation: Things we've survived seeing before are statistically safer — familiarity becomes a rough proxy for safety.

sources.title: Attitudinal effects of mere exposure
sources.publisher: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology Monographs

sources.title: Social Pressures in Informal Groups: A Study of Human Factors in Housing
